name: Selenium
contraindications: Avoid chronic high-dose intake above established upper limit due to selenosis risk

sources.title: Selenium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)
NIH cohort literature referenced in compound-research.pdf for selenium and diabetes risk context

description: Selenium is incorporated into selenoproteins involved in antioxidant biology and thyroid hormone metabolism. The corpus emphasizes established intake guidance and cautions against high-dose supplementation in non-deficient populations.
summary: Selenium is an essential trace mineral used in antioxidant and thyroid-related selenoproteins; adequacy is important, while chronic excess can cause selenosis.
mechanism: Proposed/established mix: selenium functions via selenoproteins (including glutathione peroxidase and deiodinase systems) supporting oxidative balance and thyroid hormone conversion pathways.
safetyNotes: Deficiency can contribute to clinically significant problems in low-selenium contexts.
Chronic excess intake can cause selenosis, including hair/nail changes, gastrointestinal symptoms, neuropathy, and characteristic breath odor.
UL context is approximately 400 micrograms/day for adults.
interactions: Potential interaction considerations include anticoagulant contexts and medication-level nutrient interactions requiring individualized review.
dosage: Some studies evaluated 200 micrograms/day, but this is not a universal routine recommendation.
UL context: approximately 400 micrograms/day for adults.
preparation: Common supplemental forms include selenomethionine, selenium-enriched yeast, and inorganic salts such as selenite/selenate.
regulatoryStatus: Essential trace nutrient with established dietary reference intakes; permitted in dietary supplement products.
